original_abstract: Abstract Large language model (LLM) systems, such as ChatGPT 1 or Gemini 2 , can show impressive reasoning and question-answering capabilities but often ‘hallucinate’ false outputs and unsubstantiated answers 3,4 . Answering unreliably or without the necessary information prevents adoption in diverse fields, with problems including fabrication of legal precedents 5 or untrue facts in news articles 6 and even posing a risk to human life in medical domains such as radiology 7 . Encouraging truthfulness through supervision or reinforcement has been only partially successful 8 . Researchers need a general method for detecting hallucinations in LLMs that works even with new and unseen questions to which humans might not know the answer. Here we develop new methods grounded in statistics, proposing entropy-based uncertainty estimators for LLMs to detect a subset of hallucinations—confabulations—which are arbitrary and incorrect generations. Our method addresses the fact that one idea can be expressed in many ways by computing uncertainty at the level of meaning rather than specific sequences of words. Our method works across datasets and tasks without a priori knowledge of the task, requires no task-specific data and robustly generalizes to new tasks not seen before. By detecting when a prompt is likely to produce a confabulation, our method helps users understand when they must take extra care with LLMs and opens up new possibilities for using LLMs that are otherwise prevented by their unreliability.
humanized_abstract: Large language model systems such as ChatGPT 1 or Gemini 2 demonstrate advanced reasoning and question answering capabilities, yet are prone to “hallucination” where the model generates false or unsupported answers 3,4 . The lack of reliable responses or required information precludes the adoption of such models for a wide variety of applications. Potential applications and limitations are diverse including for example the generation of legal case law that are not valid 5, generating fake information in news articles 6 and are even reported to have negatively impacted healthcare, for example in radiology 7 . However, incentivizing truthful answers via supervision and/or reinforcement learning have shown only limited success 8 . Therefore, what is needed is a general metric for assessing the degree of hallucination in large language models that is generalizable to a wide range of questions, and importantly that holds for questions to which the true answer is currently unknown to humans. Here we describe three statistically motivated methods to estimate uncertainty for large language models for a subset of hallucinations commonly referred to as confabulations: statements that are made up and do not correspond to any actual text in the training set. These methods operate at the level of meaning rather than individual word sequences, meaning that a particular word can have high entropy in one context but low in another. Importantly, they are also: 1) Dataset and task agnostic: Our models require no prior knowledge of either the training dataset, nor specifics of the task the model is being used for. 2) Unsupervised and requiring no task-specific training data Our metrics can be used to evaluate and compare models based solely on their unconditioned outputs, which makes them very generally applicable 3) Highly generalizable to unseen tasks as our model requires no training or knowledge of the target application. These properties make the models easy to use, adaptable and generally applicable for wide variety of use cases. They can be used to identify cases in which they may be critical for a user to either scrutinize closely the output given by the LLM or at least to recognize the inherent limitations of a particular response given by the system.
